title: rustc cascade root-cause identification
prompt:
task_id=T5
A Rust build just failed with 8 separate compile errors. Exactly ONE of them is the root cause — the other 7 are downstream effects the compiler reported as a side-effect of the root. Identify the 1-based index (1..8) of the root error so the developer fixes exactly that error and everything else disappears on the next build.

You must decide which error to cite. The errors differ in kind (type, trait, borrow, lifetime, resolution), location (line numbers), and which symbols they reference. Figure out what makes a root different from a cascade effect by probing the data.

Available tools:
- error_list()              — one-line-per-error banner list: `1. error[E0308] mismatched types`, etc.
- error_detail(index)       — full multi-line block for error N (spans + help text).
- error_span(index)         — just the underlined source lines for error N.
- check_error_code(code)    — rustc-explain text for an error code.
- cross_reference(a, b)     — return whether errors A and B refer to the same symbol/type/name.
- sort_by_line()            — errors re-sorted by source line number (earliest first).
- sort_by_severity()        — errors re-sorted by a rough severity heuristic (resolution > borrow > type).
- crate_imports()           — the top-of-file `use` statements in the failing module.

Call `recall_knowledge(task_id="T5")` first. If a prior run learned a procedure that names the decisive probe, apply it immediately and answer. Otherwise probe what you need — but don't burn iterations on every error when a single probe might expose the pattern.

Answer via `answer(content="<index>")` with ONLY the 1-based index as a plain integer (e.g. `3`) — no prose, no quotes, no JSON wrapping.

errors.full_text:
error[E0308]: mismatched types
  --> src/handler.rs:42:18
   |
42 |     let body: Value = parse_body(&req);
   |                  ^^^^^ expected `()`, found opaque type
   |
help: consider using `_` as the type if you don't know the concrete type

errors.code_line: error[E0277]: the trait bound `T: Serialize` is not satisfied
errors.full_text:
error[E0277]: the trait bound `T: serde::Serialize` is not satisfied
  --> src/handler.rs:47:24
   |
47 |     serde_json::to_string(&body)?;
   |                            ^^^^ the trait `Serialize` is not implemented for `T`
   |
help: consider further restricting this bound: `+ serde::Serialize`

errors.code_line: error[E0433]: failed to resolve: use of undeclared type `Value`

errors.full_text:
error[E0308]: mismatched types
  --> src/handler.rs:45:9
   |
45 |         body
   |         ^^^^ expected `Value`, found `()`

errors.code_line: error[E0277]: the trait bound `Value: Deserialize<'_>` is not satisfied
errors.full_text:
error[E0277]: the trait bound `Value: serde::Deserialize<'_>` is not satisfied
  --> src/handler.rs:50:30
   |
50 |     let parsed: Value = serde_json::from_str(&raw)?;
   |                              ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ the trait `Deserialize` is not implemented for `Value`

error[E0308]: mismatched types
  --> src/handler.rs:52:20
   |
52 |     return Ok(parsed);
   |               ^^^^^^ expected `Value`, found type that could not be resolved

error[E0277]: `Value` doesn't implement `Debug`
  --> src/handler.rs:55:24
   |
55 |     println!("{:?}", body);
   |                      ^^^^ `Value` cannot be formatted using `{:?}`
